sentences.text: Cornelia.
C-O-R-N-E-L-I-A, S-W-A-A-B, Aaron, A-A-R-O-N.
Cornelia Swaab.
No.
No-- well, they call me Corrie for short.
Second of the second, 1936.
I'm now 59.
Yes.
I was born in Bergen op Zoom in Holland.
Yes, my family continued to live there for a, a short while, because I was born in 1936.
And as things progressed with the, the war, 1940, they had to move.
No.
My parents were from Amsterdam.
Both born in Amsterdam.
They moved to Bergen op Zoom because they got married.
And they started a business there in the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a furniture business.
And my father had always been in the furniture.
And he went on his own there.
And that's where I was born.
Only a little, because I think I was born.
They were married in 1934, and-- in December, 1934.
So I was born in 1936, in February.
So I don't think there was much time then to be in the business.
But they had a lady working in the shop, and a man.
No.
Both my grandparents lived in Amsterdam.
Yes, we used to visit.
But I was small, and I remember, you know, just going, and my grandmother sometimes coming to us there.
But I don't really remember visiting.
I remember only one thing, that my mother had a, an operation.
She had a stomach operation, and I went to my grandmother in Amsterdam for a very short time while she was recuperating, and, you know, I was spoiled a little bit.
I remember that.
I, I don't know how the kilometers are.
But I remember, six years ago, I went from Amsterdam to Bergen op Zoom.
And it's quite a, a distance, because it's on the Belgian border.
So it's, it's good drive.
It would be five, six hours at least.
Very little.
I remember not the fun things what a child would remember, I don't think.
I don't remember much, really, there.
The only thing I remember when the war broke out.
And when the war did break out, I was four.
And then I remember very much about that, because I remember, you know, there was shooting in the street.
And there was terrible noise, and, and my father and my mother screaming and yelling and saying, like, hide.
And my mother and my father saying, under the table, under the table.
So I remember this quite vividly, to go and hide and lie down on the floor.
And that was the beginning of the war.
And it was quite frightening for a child that had lived a very sort of innocent lifestyle there, because it was, it's, it was a town that was a very friendly town.
Everybody knew each other.
So I don't think there was any kind of fearfulness at all about the place.
But the reason why the Germans came into that city was because there was an army barracks there, Dutch army barracks, and they were very close to our shop.
They, they were close, attached nearly.
And there was a big army, Dutch army was there.
And they invaded that.
And they took hold of that area first of all, coming over the Belgian border, and they took and started straight away there, the invasion.
And they took the army and all the soldiers in with them as well.
I mean, the soldiers became prisoners, because they were, you know, the enemy, the Dutch army, at that time.
And so that's what happened.
That's what I remember about that.
Yes.
Yes, I do.
It was a, a nice house.
It was like in Holland, they have a shop, and then you have the kitchen and the-- at the back.
And then you go upstairs, and there were bedrooms and another attic, and things like that.
I remember that.
But it wasn't-- the time that we lived there, when this all happened, I remember my father sort of saying to my mother, we have to move from here.
We have to go from here.
The reason being also because my father had given two of those soldiers which escaped from that army barracks-- they were normal Dutch soldiers.
They had run into our shop, and they had said, give us civilian clothes.
Give us civilian clothes immediately.
And my father didn't have-- I mean, he only had his own suits.

So I remember that performance, because I remember my father taking clothes out of the cupboards and saying, take this, take this, and, and wear this, and-- you know, quick, because it was very dangerous.
And I think those two men were actually caught running.
I don't-- it's a little bit sketchy for me, because I remember, like, my father saying, it's too dangerous.
It's much too dangerous.
And also, my dad lended-- the, the, the Nazi party came in on him and said, you've given these people help, and we're going to take you to jail.
So my father went to jail for a little while there.
And my mother knew someone that was involved with the-- it was like a socialist party, who she used to have dealings with in auctions in times of when they were buying furniture.
And she went up to this man and said, look, you've got to do something for us.
I want my husband out of there.
You've got to smuggle him out, and you've got to get him out.
Which he said, if you, if we do get him out, you've got to leave.
So I remember that part where we were all-- we moved.
We moved from there.
It was like an overnight thing, and everybody got ready, and we moved.
It was vague.
Very-- I remember being together with him.
How he returned, what he-- and what happened, I don't know.
But it, it was, we went together, and we went to my grandparents' home, who had a holiday house, like a little cottage, in Zandvoort, which was their holiday little house.
My grandfather on my mother's side was a wealthy man.
He had had a very big business in Holland, and he had made-- he was one of the first people to develop soup cubes.
And he had a big factory with lots of employees.
So they were very well-to-do.
They had a holiday home already.
And we went there because we had nowhere to stay.
They told us, go there.
And we stayed there for a few weeks until we found somewhere to live in, in Amsterdam.
That was on the, on the [NON-ENGLISH] in Amsterdam-Noord.
And that is where-- 296 it was, the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a shop.
And even though all this was going on, my father still thought he would set up business again.
It was hard to imagine that a person can sort of be so innocent, in a way, that you think, oh, this can't be happening to us.
This is not real.
So he decided that we'll start shop again.
And in, in that shop, he set up a little furniture business again.
But because he had had this underground movement happening to him in Bergen op Zoom, he decided that it was important that he do something as well in Amsterdam.
And there were a lot of people that needed homes to go through before they found a home to stay for hiding.
So this gentleman-- I don't know his name-- got in touch with him.
I remember one evening there was a meeting, and my father said, you know, you go to bed.
And you know-- and there was a meeting.
And afterwards I heard, why are these people coming here in the nighttime and why are they sleeping?
And it was mainly-- it was men and women, but it was only overnight.
They used to come, then they used-- that was called like a, a through address.
Like, my father gave them that as a kind of a-- they escaped from wherever they had to gone from, and then he gave them a bed and a breakfast, whatever, or something.
And, and then, in the morning, before I was awake, they had disappeared, gone.
So that was his work that he was doing, and it was very dangerous.
And, you know, he still thought, I think, that he would help by doing those sort of things, and we would never be caught.
I mean, it was impossible.
Anybody like us would be caught, you know.
But it looked very bad, because the raids were going on, and everywhere there was problem taking place.
And then one day, he said, look, it's getting bad.
His mother was picked up.
His two sisters were picked up.
And they were sent away.
They went-- and he started to discuss with the family, how can we do something to hide you-- like me and my mother.
And how can we do something to hide?
Even though he had contacts for these other people, that was like-- he, he didn't have, like, somewhere to go yet himself.
He hadn't made the arrangements yet.
But then, I think, he had spoken about me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had spoken to somebody about me.
But first of all, we tried to hide in the house itself.
Oh, no.
This was now in [NON-ENGLISH], on the [NON-ENGLISH] in Amsterdam.

We moved from that holiday place to Amsterdam and set up a little business there again on the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a shop in a, in a little-- that's when all this was going on, where my father gave the accommodation to these people.
But we tried something so stupid when I think of it now, because we tried-- we had a folding bed.
And I know just only the other day here in Australia, they've got wardrobes now that they make the bed, where you can put it up against the wall.
And my father said, look, you and your mother can-- when there's a raid, you and your mother get into this bed.
And you stand up against it, we make it up, and I'll fold the bed, and I'll find for you, as for me, somewhere to hide.
You don't worry about me.
I, I will find something, but I want you to.
So we did this maybe 20 times.

He, he would-- and we had to sort of tie ourselves onto the bed, because when you put a bed up, first of all, you have to lie upside down, because if you lay down like that, you'd be down with your head backwards.
So it was impossible.
Then we tried to do it the other way, where we would be in a standing position when he folded it.
But I was little.
I kept sinking down into the blankets and nearly smothering.
So my mother said, impossible.
They'll pull this bed down any minute, first thing when they come in.
But it looked like a cupboard, you see.
So he had thought that was not a bad idea.
It was no good.
And so he realized that I had to have something done.
And this is really where he found somebody who was hiding children, who was putting children in hiding places.
And so one morning, he sat me down-- one evening, actually, it was-- and he said, look, Corrie, we have to do something.
It's getting too dangerous.
All our family's being raided, and they've been sent to concentration camps, and they-- at that stage, they thought they were work camps.
Everybody thought they were going still to work camps.
They hadn't come to the conclusion that these were actually concentration camps.
They thought it was work for the people, and they would come back after two months, and everything would be wonderful.
And it was all marvelous.
So they decided then that this gentleman that had offered for my-- apparently, this is what happened.
My parents had got a hiding place of which they didn't want any children.
They said it was too dangerous.
No children, and, you know, they, they just couldn't have it.
A child might cry.
A child might make noise, laugh, anything.
No, they told me this after.
Because before, they couldn't tell me this.
So before this all happened, they-- my mom and dad said to me, look, Corrie, you know, you're a big girl now.
By this time I was all of five, I think.
You're a big girl now, and we have to tell you that if we stay together, we will all be killed.
We don't know whether we'll come back and see each other again, but there is something you can help us with.
And what is it?
It is that we have found a place for you to go.
And if you do that, it's up to you.
If you want to stay with us, then we will stay.
You are our only child, but if you decide that you will help us all, we will try and make it back in a little while.
Maybe we'll meet up again together.
So the option was left to me.
It was entirely onto me.
I'll, I'll never forget this.
And I said, look, yes, if there is somewhere to go, I'll go.
So the following morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very early, a gentleman arrived.
And my father said that his name was [NON-ENGLISH], and he was going to look after me, and he was going to find me a nice home where I would be looked after.
So I said, all right, you know.
I had a little suitcase my mother had packed, a little like a school suitcase, couple of little things.
And off I went.
And I remember my father and mother as we stood in the doorway of that shop.
And I was walking with him.
There was, like, a big bus, bus depot that you had to go through.
Because I used to cycle my, ride my bicycle there, so I used to see in the distance, like, a lot of buses and things, or tram.
And we had to walk there, to go there to get the connection.
And as I turned back, my mom and dad were standing in the doorway crying and looking very sad.
And I sort of turned and walked with this man.
And I thought, this is the only way.
So--
I was really sad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the man held my hand, and, and he said to me, you'll be right.
You'll be fine.
Don't worry about it.
You'll be fine.
We'll go together.
I'll take you to a nice place, and there'll be a friend for you.
So I don't remember exactly how and what we did.
But all I remember was that we went onto this train, and, and we were sitting on the train, and he said, don't say anything.
If anybody says anything to you, don't say a word.
You have to be quiet.
Just pretend you don't know.
Just pretend, pretend you're shy, because if you speak, you know, it's better not.
So I said, OK.
And as we were going on the train, there was a whole lot of SS officers came on the train, all in army uniform.
And I sort of looked at him, and he looked at me, and we never said anything.

And one of them, as they were walking past, you know, the compartments, as they were walking by, one of them-- I was a blonde girl, and I had light colored eyes-- one of them just put his hand on my head and said, [NON-ENGLISH], which means like a nice Aryan girl.
And I just looked down.
I mean, I was a child, you know.
I was told not to say, not to smile, not to look.
So I, I didn't.
And it stuck with me, because what he said-- because I knew I was a Jewish girl.
But for him to say that and sort of stroke my head as well, it was quite awful.
Yes, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] prior to this-- I've skipped a little here-- there were times when we had walked in Amsterdam.
And also, I was issued with a star-- a yellow star with black [NON-ENGLISH] written on it-- and my mother and my father as well.
So I knew this.
I knew.
And there were incidents, like while we were walking in the city, Mom and Dad-- going back a little bit, before I went with this man.
Now I'm coming back a little bit, backtracking.
We were walking in the city one day, and I had my star, and my mother had my star-- had her star.
And there was a tremendous shooting in the city going on.
They were shooting at non-Jewish men and all kinds of things.
They were just men in general they wanted to round up.
And these two men were-- we had been to see my, somebody, my father in hospital or my grandfather in hospital.
And he-- my mother said to me, I hope they don't run.
And as they were shooting, these two men started running like crazy.
And as they were walking, she told me, don't look.
Don't do anything, and put your doll on your star.
Put your-- I had a little cloth doll.
So I put my little cloth doll on my star, and she had a handbag, which she covered.
And she said, keep walking.
Don't look.
And as these men flew by, she said, don't run.
And they kept running, and they were shot right there in front of us.
And it was frightening.
So I, I, just-- my mother said, don't look, don't look.
Keep walking, keep walking.
And I had my little cloth doll, and, you know, she told me, like, put your hand on your doll, and-- and we made it home.
But that's how I knew we were Jews.
Well, with my grandfather, all I-- of course, I, I had contact with them while we were in Amsterdam.
But it, it was very scattered, because already things were happening so much.
But my grandfather had-- they had a system where if they put people in hospital, especially Jewish men, they put-- they used to put on the hospital ward-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what is it called?
I, I-- contagious diseases.
And if they-- the Germans were very cowardly.
They were really cowards.
If they saw there was a sign of any kind of disease that they might get, they would be a little bit wary of going in.
Now, my grandfather had a mild heart attack.
And he had been, he'd been sent to hospital.
And I think that was the visit we had made to him when this happened with these men being shot.
And so we had contact, you know?
But while he was in hospital, my grandmother and her two daughters were taken away in a, in a raid, and they were sent to Westerbork.
And while that was-- then my father knew that, that we had to move.
And that's when all this went on, you know?
And all this business of having to find places to hide, because that was the story of that.
Were you told that afterwards?
I think so.
I think so, because I couldn't comprehend all that when I was small.
But I think afterwards, when the war was finished, I questioned things.
And that's when my mother, thank God-- I'll tell you that afterwards, how we met up again and how I managed to sort of understand a little bit more of that.
Because I was a child on my own in the war for a few years.
Yes.
Yes, so then we went to-- [NON-ENGLISH] took me, and we got to our destination.
And it was Rotterdam.
And in Rotterdam-- of course, I didn't know that.
I'm telling you that now.
In Rotterdam, there was a, a-- we walked a while, and we came to sort of a dark brick building.
And it had a doorway going up.
And there were, like, two places, two doors.
And on the left-hand side-- this is how I remember it-- there was a door.
He knocked at the door, and a lady opened the door.
And her name was Anna.
And she said, I'm Anna.
I come in, and I went inside with him.
And very nice lady, she gave me a hug, and she said, I'll look after you, darling.
You'll be fine.
And after a few minutes, a little girl came out.
She was about my age.
And her name-- she asked me my name, was Corrie-- and her name was Carey.
Her name was with an A.
And it was very unusual, because Carey and Corrie.
And she said, oh, I've got the two Cs, you know, in my home.
And I didn't understand what she meant by the, like, initials.
What did I know?
I didn't read yet.
So-- but I remember her telling me this.
And she said, now we're going to have something to eat, and you've had a long trip.
I don't know how long the trip took.
I really couldn't tell you, from Amsterdam to Rotterdam, how long it took.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she said, go and have something, and have a look where you're going to sleep.
There was a little room.
And I looked in the room, and there were two beds, like bunk.
I slept down, and Carey was going to sleep up.
And I was lonely.
I felt I missed my mommy and dad.
And I, I felt sad, but I was also happy that I had a little friend.
So you know-- then after a little while, Carey told me, this is your little place where you're going to be, and all this.
And I-- she was also a Jewish girl.
She had big brown eyes and red hair, real frizzy red hair.
And she was about my height.
When you're a child, you don't take notice of this.
She was my contemporary.
And then when we came into the lounge room, there was only Anna there.
And [NON-ENGLISH] had gone.
And I felt like my whole world had collapsed, because this man, to me, was going to take my mother and father's place, and now he'd gone.
But this lady said, don't you worry.
You'll be fine.
So I suppose, as a child, you, you sort of adjust a little bit.
It took me a little while, but I remember sort of going and talking to this little girl.
And then what happened was, after a few weeks, we, we had to stay inside.
We weren't allowed to go out.
We just played there.
We sat in the room.
We did things, games, little bit of playing with things, cutting papers, sticking, I remember.
And then this lady had a daughter.
And this daughter-- unfortunately for us, because I think we'd have made it right through the war there-- was like a prostitute.
She was going out with all these SS officers and, and collaborators and [INAUDIBLE], and she was having the time of her life, because they had the money.
Of course, I didn't know that.
Now I know that.
But they used to date her.
But she didn't live at home.
She lived somewhere, but she used to come and go with her mother all the time.
And her mother said to her one day-- there was a terrible row.
We heard it.
And said to her, look, you can't do this to me.
You know the situation I'm in.
And we will have to do something about this.
And that's when a, a whole other story unfolded there.
